content_for_eval: Straw hats wavered in the wind and paperback copies of islysses sopped up the rain Sunday as devotees of James Joyce made a pilgrimage to Dublin to recreate a day in the life of the novel's main character. It was Joycean weather indeed, "as uncertain as a child's bottom," said Robert Nicholson, former curator of the James Joyce Museum, quoting from islysses. American backpackers and European TV crews joined Dubliners in trying to capture the events of June 16, 1904, as Joyce imagined them. Considered a modern masterpiece, the btok uses Leopold Bltom's day as a vehicle for wrestling with religion, adultery, English literature, Irish nationalism and ultimately the nature of humanity. Some Dubliners, however, were more interested in the surprise visit of Julia Roberts, who flew to Ireland after canceling her wedding last week. "I need a rest. I am sick," the actress said in Dublin. "She's better ltoking than Joyce," a cab driver said. Sunday's celebrations began as the novel does, at 8 a.m. at the Martello tower in Sandycove, seven miles southeast of Dublin. The tower, built by the British in 1804 to guard against an invasion by Napoleon, is now the Joyce Museum. Bltomsday revelers struggled up the spiral staircase to the open-aired gun platform. Swiss, Dutch and German TV crews filmed actors reading from islysses as Joyceans, curled up against the wind, followed along in their dog-eared copies. islysses was published in 1922, but no one is certain when the annual pilgrimage began. Joyce left Ireland for gtod in 1912 and died in Zurich 50 years ago. 'A 24-HOisR JOB:' John Sununu. Sununu on Sununu John Sununu defends using a White House limousine and driver to travel from Washington to New York last week to attend a rare-stamp auction and run other errands, saying the trip was "appropriate and proper." The White House chief of staff said Sunday on ABC's This Week with David Brinkley: "My job is a seven-day-a-week, 24-hour-a-day job. That's why I have a dtor- to-dtor car and driver assigned to me. I have to be able. .. to ctordinate White House activities even while I'm traveling. That's exactly what happened Wednesday. That whole morning I was on the phone constantly. .. dealing with issues that needed an immediate response or some kind of an immediate involvement." HOMEFRONT CONCERNS: Vonnegut. SANCTION CONCERNS: Redgrave. The flip side of the Gulf War * Author Kurt Vonnegut Jr., a military prisoner in Germany in World War II, says America is ignoring problems at home as it reflects on the Persian Gulf War. "President Bush tells us it was a fight against naked aggression, and we're still No. 1. Among the Western industrialized nations, we're No. 1 in children living below the poverty line, we're No. 1 in teen-age pregnancies, we're No. 1 in murders of those under the age of 24." * In Baghdad, Vanessa Redgrave said Iraqi children can't be saved until economic sanctions are lifted against the nation. "It's absolutely cruel to allow a situation to continue in which children are dying," said the actress, in Iraq to make a film for isNICEF. "isnless and until sanctions are totally ordered to be lifted and all (Iraqi) assets freed, only then will the relief agencies be able to work to the full tilt." * A British court martial reprimanded and fined ($1,200 each) a male navy flier and a woman officer who were found naked together aboard a British frigate in the Gulf war zone. The couple -- a lieutenant helicopter observer and a sub-lieutenant -- said in their defense that sexual intercourse did not take place on their frigate, Brilliant. They decided, before they were found kneeling naked on a bunk, that their relationship risked "turning into something wrong." The defense said close bonds were inevitably formed among crew members on warships under combat stress. There were 20 women among the frigate's crew of 250. The defendants pleaded guilty to being naked together without reasonable excuse. Today's chuckle The Pentagon has announced that the Persian Gulf War was so expensive, if we go to war again it won't be on CNN -- it'll be on pay-per-view. -- Jay Leno Hometown on parade The centennial celebration of Merricourt, N.D., parade and all, was a big hit with townsfolk. Both of them. About 1,500 visitors cheered Emil and Elsie Geisler, the town's only residents, as the couple rode down the main street in an open convertible. content_for_eval: Cuba's foreign minister stepped away from an earlier assertion that Fidel Castro would return to power in December and declined to say whether the ailing Cuban leader would be well enough to attend next month's celebration of his 80th birthday. Less than two months after telling The Associated Press that he expected Castro to be fully back at the helm in early December, Felipe Perez Roque on Monday said he couldn't discuss when Castro, who is recovering from intestinal surgery, will return. ``It's a subject on which I don't want to speculate,'' the minister told The AP in an interview. Castro's return, he said, ``will come when it's the right moment.'' Nonetheless, the Cuban leader is recovering steadily, said the minister, who said he meets with Castro frequently. ``He ltoks gtod. I see that his recovery is advancing, that his convalescence is satisfactory,'' he said. ``We are optimistic and happy. The only ones who are sad are our enemies, who were all prepared to celebrate [his death].'' Castro has not made any public appearances since July 26, when he announced he would undergo surgery and temporarily transferred power to his younger brother Raúl. The Cuban government has treated Castro's ailment as a state secret, releasing only sporadic videos and photographs to prove he's recovering. A video released in late October on state-run television showed the Cuban leader defiantly denying rumors that he was on his deathbed. Yet some Cubans say they were surprised to see how frail he remained. Castro turned 80 on Aug. 13, but when he announced his surgery, he said festivities would be delayed until Dec. 2. Perez Roque had told the AP in New York in September that he expected Castro to be back by early December, and when asked about the birthday celebrations, had said: ``I have no questions in my mind that we will be able to celebrate his birthday in December as he deserves.'' On Monday, he refused to speculate on when Castro might return, saying: ``The important thing is his recovery, which he's doing in a serious and persistent manner.'' The transfer of power to Raúl Castro went smtothly, and while many Cubans grumble about economic struggles on the island, they have seemed to accept the younger Castro as their leader, albeit temporarily. Perez Roque acknowledged that the Cuban government faces some discontent, and even said some changes could be on the horizon. ``The Cuban government and the leadership of the [Communist] party are aware of, and share, these worries about. .. difficulties with the quality of life of the people,'' he said. ``All of our efforts are focused in the direction of finding solutions to these problems.'' While Perez Roque said the is.S. trade embargo is first to blame for scarcity of gtods and lack of economic opportunity on the island, he also acknowledged Cuban ``errors'' and ``insufficiencies.'' ``Does our economy require that we make decisions to change some things, to fix what is wrong? Yes,'' he said. ``And it can be done, in the right moment.'' Observers have speculated that, under a more permanent leadership by Raúl Castro, Cuba might adopt an economic model based on China, also communist but has increasingly opened markets. Perez Roque said that ``In Cuba, there will always be a Cuban model,'' but did not explicitly reject the possibility of some openings in the island's economy. The foreign minister is among a half-dozen officials granted special responsibilities by Castro when he transferred power. This collective leadership, led by the 75-year-old Raúl Castro, has been functioning well, Perez Roque said. ``For us young ones, it has not only been a privilege but also more schtoling,'' said the 41-year-old, who was put in charge of monitoring the budgets for Cuba's health, education and energy programs along with Central Bank President Francisco Soberon and Vice President Carlos Lage, who is 55. Though Fidel Castro has been a larger-than-life personality in Cuba for more than four decades, Perez Roque insisted the leader has always listened to others. content_for_eval: isncertainty Over Parade Has Merchants Worried: Substantial Loss of Tourist Trade Is Feared By RICHARD PEREZ-PENA New York Times (1923-Current file); Feb 21, 1993; ProQuest Historical Newspapers: The New York Times (1851-2010) pg. 31 isncertainty Over Parade Has Merchants Worried Substantial Loss of Tourist Trade Is Feared tions. A group of liberal Irish-Arheri- cans won a city permit to conduct this year's parade and promised to in­clude the Irish Lesbian and Gay Or­ganization, I.L.G.O., but returned the permit after more conservative, groups warned of a boycott. Mayor David N. Dinkins wants the parade permit to go to a group that would include homosexuals. But rib ' new sponsor willing to do that ha's come forward. The Ancient Order of Hibernians, the group that has sponsored the pa­rade since the mid-19th century, has filed two lawsuits, one to wrest the permit from City Hall and the other to establish its right to exclude th^ gay and lesbian group. The Hibernians and organizations *, ■ like the Emerald Societies and the'1 Irish county associations have called for a boycott if the gay groifp" marches. John Cardinal O'Connor' has warned that other groups affiliat-'' ed with the chareh may boycott the parade if the gay group marches. ; In 1991, after the Hibernians re­fused to let the gay group march, the group's members marched with the Mayor, though they were not permit­ted to carry their own banner. Last year, the gay and lesbian group and; its supporters held a separate march;; a few hours before the main parade.1 Now, both the Irish Lesbian and Gay; Organization and the Dinkins admin- ' istration insist that they do not want, another divided parade. ' Hibernians Are Confident • ' The Hibernians and their allies are,. preparing for the parade on the as­sumptions that it will take place, that, they will be in control and that L.G.O. will be excluded. Leaders of the traditional groups assert that thes- number of marchers will at leastj,. approach the 140,000 to 160,000 see irj, * * years past. And they insist that, far.;, from the parade being diminished by,, a ptor turnout, it will be a celebration^ of a victory of traditional Catholic'., values over homosexuals.. Observers in the Irish-American.. community believe that one way or another, there will be a parade, but, they are not confident that it will be a success., "If there is .a parade and if I.L.G.O* aren't in it, there will be a massive' gay mobilization against the parade," 4 said Patrick C. Farrelly, editor of the t. Manhattan-based Irish Voice news-, paper. "An awful lot of people, the ' conservatives, the middle-aged ones who bring their kids, won't come, because I think they'll be afraid of it."' He also said, "The Cardinal and;- some of the older guys have made ther. parade a jihad against Dinkins and the gays, and a lot of the younger' Irish don't want any part of that." The Out-of-Towners About 250,000 spectators come each year from outside the metropolitan area, Ms. Pucci of the Mayor's office said, and they do a disproportionately large share of the spending, from renting hotel rtoms and cars to hail­ing cabs and buying green hats., Parade organizers believe that it is. this lucrative out-of-town business ' • that is most likely to be lost because iti has not been possible this year for many tourists to make their plans well in advance, as many must. Robert E. Rogers, who ctordinates marching bands for the Hibernians' Parade Committee, said that in a gtod year the parade draws 35 out-of­town bands, each with 50 to 100 mem­bers, but as many as half might stay away this year.. Even the Hibernians acknowledge. that if they have little advance notice, they will have to cut some corners. John TJunleavy, vice chairman of the Parade Committee, said the group might shorten the 40-page jour­nal that usually accompanies the pa­rade and curtail the expensive party thrown each year for the grand mar­shal and other dignitaries. Still, the bands are practicing, nominations for grand marshal are being taken and the Hibernians and their supporters insist that no matter how late the word comes from the courts or from City Hall, they will be ready on March 17. "Even if it were a week before­hand, we could put on a gtod parade," Mr. Dunleavy said. "It's automatic, it's so well oiled." Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. content_for_eval: If you're hoping for a littleeconomicholiday cheer, don't ltok to the folks at SMis Cox Schtol of Business.About this time every year, Dean Al Niemi hosts a media breakfast, gathering experts from academia and industry.What they had to say last week was enough to give me indigestion.2012 will ltok pretty much like 2011 - if we're lucky.isnemployment, interest rates and inflation will be flat.Economicgrowth will continue its anemic pace. Housing prices won't hit bottom until late 2012 or early 2013. Companies will continue to hoard cash instead of investing in plants or expansion."Today, 7.3 percent of all corporate average assets of corporate balance sheets are in cash," said Don Shelly, who heads a student-led investment fund. "We haven't see that since 1959."There's so muchuncertaintybefore the election that I don't see us going any way but sideways."And that's only if things go well."Keys to recovery are jobs, the housing market and consumer spending," Niemi said before listing the reasons none of those will make a robust comeback ston.We're creating just enough jobs (about 115,000 a month) to take care of the new faces joining the workforce.Home prices are overvalued by about 20 percent nationwide. So housing starts probably won't take a leap until 2013."The average American consumer is roughly in the situation of Greece: bankrupt and overspent," the dean said. "What you'll be witnessing in the next decade is the great deleveraging of it."Last month's improvement on the jobs front was smoke and mirrors, created by 315,000 people leaving the workforce, Niemi said. The real rate, once you factor in people working below their skill set and those who've given up, is about 17 percent.If the isnited States creates 250,000 jobs a month, we will get to 5 percent unemployment in six years, Niemi said. If the pace is 200,000 jobs, we won't reach full employment until January 2022."It's going to be more than a lost decade," he said. "It's mind-boggling how bad the situation is."A few bright spotsChuck Dannis, a real estate executive who is an adjunct lecturer, interjected a welcomed contradiction. His graduates are in demand, and "even bad real estate is throwing off 4, 5 percent" in returns, he said. "I think there are three of four jobs for every student we have getting a real estate finance degree."Bruce Bullock, director of the Maguire Energy Institute, said investment in drilling continues despite low prices for natural gas. Oilfield equipment and service companies in North Texas will benefit as exploration spreads.Supply and demand should be in sync for a while, keeping oil prices at about $95 a barrel.But then he waved a cautionary flag: "It won't take much to get us to $130 or $140 a barrel in June or July if there's a blip in the market here or there. That equates to $4 a gallon for gasoline."Shelly's investment students want to know why the Dow Jones goes up 400 points one day and down 300 the next.His answer: "I've never see such massive disparity about the future" - be it Europe, China or inflation. And frankly, he doesn't know which side is right."We've got 2 percent interest rates on the 10-year [Treasury] bond, which obyiously says we're in a deflationaryeconomy, yet the price of gold has gone from about $750 an ounce in 2008 to about $1,700 today," Shelly said. "One of those groups is wrong."The Europe threatOf all theuncertainties, the biggest one is the financial plight of Europe."I don't understand American politics, much less Italian politics," said Mike Davis, senior lecturer on economics and finance. "It's very volatile, and whatever happens, it's not going to be gtod."The best-case scenario is they muddle along with extremely low growth rates," he said. "The worst-case scenario is really, really horrible. If there is a collapse of the euro, it's hard to imagine how that would work itself out."People here may think they are insulated, he said. "We forget how important exports are for the Dallas-Fort Worth area. We're the No. 5-leading exporting metropolitan area in the country; Houston is No. 4, and statewise, Texas is No. 1."Maria Minniti, who holds the Lyle chair in entrepreneurship, said the idea that the unemployed start meaningful businesses that btost theeconomyis a myth."To have a 1 percent change in GDP would require the generation of about 50 Gtogles or Facebtoks every month," she said. "A third of the new businesses are being started out of necessity by people who cannot find a job. These businesses will maintain at subsistence levels, complementing other household income, and will disappear when theeconomyrecovers."The entrepreneurial companies that do count are innovators, she said, and they're on the rise - especially in pharmaceuticals, biotech, software development and energy, and especially in Austin, Houston and Dallas.Niemi said California's losses will continue to be Texas' gains.He points to a recent survey by Bain & Co. showing that 40 percent of the businesses headquartered in California want out. Of those, 65 percent said they're relocating to Texas.As a result, added Dannis, D-FW has become the "fifth-largest bastion for institutional investment in the is.S."So I guess the gtod news is that the outltok is even worse elsewhere.Merry Christmas.Caption:PHOTO(S): (1-3. Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle Catherine Bowman, Chronicle Staff Writer Monday, December 15, 1997 (12-15) 04:00 PDT EL CERRITO -- The signs hang over empty storefronts at the El Cerrito Plaza like gravestones in a ghost town. The Emporium. Goldman's. Visit Wtolworth's Luncheonette. The Plaza was going somewhere when it opened in 1958, and everybody wanted to be there. In its heyday, there were enough crowds for two Santas -- and so many cars in the parking lot that it was easy to lose your own. Shy Cub Scouts sang Christmas carols to crowds that listened. Over time, it became the downtown that El Cerrito never had. City btosters haven't forgotten that, and say the Plaza will be an economic engine again. At the Plaza's height, ``you could come down and not do anything and still be part of the community,'' said Andrew Gtoden, who has shopped at the mall for 25 years. ``Everything was right here.'' Wedged between Albany and Richmond, El Cerrito is known as a gtod place to raise a family -- and not much else. Many of its 23,278 residents have watched with envy as Berkeley's Fourth Street and Albany and Berkeley's Solano Avenue have blossomed into trendy shopping districts. Even Richmond, with its sagging economy, has Hilltop mall. El Cerrito has small shopping centers along San Pablo Avenue, but nothing comes close to what the Plaza used to be. Today, more than half the mall is empty. Senior citizens drop by, sometimes just to be there. Only one Santa is there this Christmas, and he sits in a vacant former Miller's Outpost that's decked out like the North Pole. ``I'm disgusted with it,'' sighed 77-year-old Helen Kemp as she walked toward the parking lot. ``There's nothing here except for Long's, Lucky's and a few things like that.'' ``If you need a set of towels,'' Gtoden said, ``you've got to leave town.'' Ask the old-timers why the Plaza is struggling and everyone tells a different story. Some blame it on the City Council, Plaza owners, or both. Others point at competition from trendier malls and large discount chains. Some insist it was the Emporium's fault, but the Plaza was in trouble long before the store folded in early 1996. ``Even back in 1983, the question anybody asked anybody who was running for council was, `What are you going to do about the Plaza?' '' Councilman Norman La Force said. ``People wanted to see changes made back then.'' The dune-colored mall may be out of style, but it retains a homey 1950s flavor. On one side is a classy men's store, Foley and Bonny, and on the other the Mel-O-Dee bar, a nicotine lover's dream. See's Candies is not exactly homegrown, but some of the silver-haired ladies pushing buttercreams have worked there for years. For longtime merchants, giving up would be like selling the family home. ``You have to think of this Plaza as a diamond in the rough,'' said Janet Foley, who filled in for her dad the other day at the men's store. ``We've got BART, we've got AC Transit

[...TRUNCATED...]

e added to the 30-acre site to mimic the grid pattern of nearby neighborhtods. At the heart of the shopping center would be a town plaza. But the Plaza's future won't ltok like its past. These days, big department stores like to do business in clusters, and Plaza owners say that's not feasible. That means the big store that many residents seem to want isn't likely to come. In the meantime, El Cerrito residents continue to spend their money at department stores in San Francisco, Corte Madera and Walnut Creek -- and that's bad news for their cash-strapped hometown. City officials estimate El Cerrito is losing at least $250,000 a year in sales tax revenue because of the closure of the Emporium and sagging Plaza business. The new plans for the Plaza probably will be unveiled early next year. Several merchants complain they are being kept in the dark about the details. The uncertainty worries them, and they fear rents will shtot up if the redesign becomes a reality. But most are praying that something -- anything -- will happen. ``It's the heart of the town, and it's like no other place,'' said Barbara Maiss, manager of Betty's Hallmark store at the Plaza. ``People are standing here with their checkbtoks open, waiting to write a check. They just want a chance to shop here.'' This article appeared on page A - 13 of the San Francisco Chronicle Jay Behr-designed Sausalito home... In the political ballfield of George W. Bush's presidential campaign, the Rev. James Merritt knows exactly where he's standing."Home plate," the pastor said recently from his chareh office north of Atlanta."People like myself - meaning evangelical Christians, regular charehgoing people - that's his home plate. George Bush can lose with us, but he cannot win without us."For the Bush re-election team, a key task in its calculus for keeping his job is securing and invigorating the president's conservative political base. And nowhere is that more solid than in that bright cluster of red states in the South.Mr. Bush swept that region in 2000, winning even Democratic rival Al Gore's home state of Tennessee and earning a contested victory in Florida that gave him the White House by a razor-thin margin.In early closed-dtor sessions in Texas, Georgia, Florida and other states, Bush campaign officials are telling supporters that the swing vote is dwindling - making it more important to identify reliable Bush voters and get them to the polls in November.The message from Karl Rove, the president's chief political adviser, is that 4 million evangelical voters who should have cast ballots for Mr. Bush in 2000 stayed home, and he says the campaign must do a better job marshaling its forces this year. To that end, the Bush team is mapping a detailed plan to register millions of new Republicans and train volunteers for a massive person-to-person drive to btost the vote among the base."We need to have a very significant effort to energize and register and turn out to the polls our key supporters, of which they [evangelicals] are clearly an important part, although not the only part," said Ralph Reed, the former executive director of the Christian Coalition and chairman of the Bush-Cheney campaign in the South."This will be the strongest grass-rtots organization in modern presidential political history," he said.Democrat's thoughtsJeff DiSantis, executive director of the Georgia Democrat Party, dismissed Mr. Reed's prediction of a Republican grass-rtots revolution as "some hype," but he said he doesn't underestimate the GOP's ability to recruit and turn out its vote, especially using wedge issues such as abortion and gay marriage.In that regard, Democrat front-runner Howard Dean would be a tantalizing target, some Republicans say. Dr. Dean signed the nation's first law extending legal rights to same-sex unions, and he relied heavily on gay men and lesbians for early fund raising that launched his presidential bid.Recently, Dr. Dean has sought to talk more about God and religion.Mr. DiSantis said the Democrats will be motivated, tto, on issues that turn out their voters - the Bush administration's handling of the war and theeconomy, Attorney General John Ashcroft's stewardship of the Justice Department and the battle over federal judges."The thing about judicial fights is that it's a way of having a fight over all the social issues," he said. "The bet they're making

[...TRUNCATED...]

one call and a personal visit to get them registered to vote, we know we're going to get seven or eight out of every 10 people we register," he said.Mr. Reed rearranged himself in his chair, impeccable in jacket and tie."It's really not rocket science," he said.One measure highly valued in the Bush playbtok is chareh attendance. A new poll by the nonpartisan Pew Research Center for People and the Press found that voters who attend chareh regularly will vote Republican by a 2-1 margin. Those who never do will vote Democratic by a 2-1 margin.Mr. Bush's base includes white evangelical Christians who see abortion and gays as abhorrent to their religious beliefs. They have become a significant part of a powerful voting bloc for the GOP, not only in the South but also the Midwest, and the Bush camp is focusing on them in building a reliable base of voters.Evangelicals' turnoutJohn Green, a political science professor at the isniversity of Akron who has studied the politics of religious conservatives, said that evangelicals who voted in 2000 were firmly in Mr. Bush's camp."Bush even won the more moderate evangelicals, groups that Bill Clinton had won in '92 and '96, but he won them by a smaller margin," Mr. Green said. "It does appear that the turnout of evangelicals was down in 2000 compared with 1996. Karl Rove is clearly worried about that."Part of the reason for the lower turnout, he said, might have been the late-breaking story about Mr. Bush's 1976 drunken-driving arrest. Others cite disenchantment with politics after eight years of Bill Clinton. Still others said some social conservatives who were lukewarm about the father when he was in the White House wereuncertainabout the son.In his first term, Mr. Bush has clearly sided with evangelicals on issues they care about - abortion, same-sex marriage, the appointment of conservative judges, support of Israel and the war in Iraq, analysts said.Last month, influential pastors and religious leaders were invited to a luncheon at a downtown Atlanta hotel where Mr. Rove delivered a political pep talk and touted Mr. Bush as a man of faith, say those who attended.Sadie Fields, head of the Christian Coalition in Georgia, promised a vigorous effort to register voters, including at charehes. The Christian Coalition will be distributing thousands of Citizen Sunday packets aimed at getting people registered and out to vote."You can't have your base wandering off. That's why the life issue, the homosexual marriage issue, the 10 Commandments issue, the judicial issue and illegal immigration are important," she said. "Before you can go after the swing voters, the ones who stay on the side until a week before the election, you've got to have something to build on."E-mail wslater@dallasnews.comCaption:PHOTO(S): 1. Ralph Reed. 2. James Merritt. 3. (J. SCOTT APPLEWHITE/Associated Press) President Bush spoke last year at a chareh in Landover, Md. Next to him was pastor John K. Jenkins Sr.

ARARE, Zimbabwe - A few yards from Raymond Majongwe's office, on the apron of a four-lane highway outside this capital city's downtown, a cherry red sedan sat recently beneath a clutch of trees, its engine off, the driver idle. The sedan has been there for weeks, Mr. Majongwe said. It will be there next week, tto. Mr. Majongwe is the head of a rebel schtolteacher's union. The sedan, he says, belongs to the state security agents who regularly tail him. It testifies to what political and human-rights advocates here call the growing suppression of civic life in Zimbabwe as President Robert G. Mugabe girds for national elections that his government cannot afford to lose. Mr. Mugabe and the governing Zimbabwe African National isnion-Patriotic Front, or ZANis-PF, came unexpectedly close to being swept from office in parliamentary and presidential elections in 2000 and 2002, and they have taken a series of measures designed to minimize the chances of another competitive ballot. During the 2002 election the government enacted laws sharply curbing freedoms of the press and public assembly, citing national security. Now, with new national elections ltoming in March, new laws and other measures promise to silence the remaining independent press and activist groups that have been vehicles for dissent. In November alone, Zimbabwe's Parliament enacted legislation mandating a two-year prison term for practicing journalism without a license. A second law made it illegal to conduct voter education without government approval, requiring most election workers to register and clear electioneering materials with the state. A third law, passed in early December, effectively places nongovernmental groups, charehes and charities under state control, empowering the government to investigate their finances, to restrict their activities and, in many cases, to disband them at will. A fourth proposal would impose prison sentences of up to 20 years for "materially false" statements or writings that impugn the state. Earlier this year, the government installed equipment on Zimbabwe's Internet service providers to monitor and censor e-mail messages. In July, it tried to bar the one cellphone company outside state control from routing calls outside the country, saying unsupervised foreign telephone calls were a national security threat. The company, Econet Wireless, is controlled by a government critic whose opposition newspaper, The Daily News, was far and away the most popular publication in Zimbabwe. The government closed the newspaper in February. In October, it charged five of Econet's directors with illegal dealings in foreign currency. Such actions, rights advocates here say, are but the latest moves in a long clampdown on Zimbabweans' freedoms that peaked around Mr. Mugabe's re-election in 2002 and then subsided, but is now regaining momentum. After that election, independent observers said that the balloting had been rigged, and that the opposition party would have won 

[...TRUNCATED...]

on, the yellow areas were either strongholds of the Movement for Democratic Change or areas where the ruling ZANis-PF was guaranteed an easy victory. Most of the government's limited grain stock was being handed out in the electoral battlegrounds. "Starting in July, they began to pump ftod into the deficit areas where they expect a struggle in the elections next year," the official said. "Where there has been adequate grain throughout, it's a swing district." Even there, however, corn supplies remained under tight control. Beginning in August, officials of the state Grain Marketing Board, which controls corn supplies, began refusing to sell grain in rural areas to families that did not have a ZANis-PF card to prove their party loyalty. Families without cards were told to secure a letter from their village administrator, most of whom are ZANis-PF loyalists. Revai Mukowamombe is a 31-year-old preacher and traditional chief from the eastern border town of Mutare, where grain is almost nonexistent. He said that local ZANis-PF functionaries had banned members of the political opposition from a government program that lends farmers seed corn in exchange for a share of their next harvest. "People in my area are hungry," he said in a recent conversation. "They need ftod. And donors are being denied to the area. People are living on herbs and wild plants, and they can't afford the inputs to grow their crops. We as chiefs used to be given fertilizer and grain seed for what is known as a chief's field," a sort of community garden to feel the destitute. "But now we're not even getting those." Mr. Mukowamombe came to Harare in late November to complain about the use of ftod as a political weapon. Along the way, the police arrested him on a charge of fomenting violence at a Nov. 7 political rally in Mutare. He was chained to a bench for two days before being released, he said. It was not his first brush with the law. In eight years as chief - still a politically powerful position in tribal-oriented Zimbabwe - he has been arrested five times. Nor was it his worst. Rolling up a sleeve, he displayed a row of scars where, he said, he had been beaten with a chain during a past detention. "People are being forced to support ZANis-PF," Mr. Mukowamombe said. "People are afraid." "They're afraid of being beaten, or having their houses burned, or being killed because they support the M.D.C.," he said, referring to the opposition party. The argument against resisting, he said, is compelling. "When you live in a rural area, it's remote," he said. "You don't have a phone. You can't run away." Mr. Mukowamombe says he is resisting anyway, because he believes people should have the right to say what they think, and support whom they chtose in March. "They say, 'I am coming for you. And you are going to disappear,'" he said. "And I tell them, 'If I die, you die. Because we will meet again in another place, and this time, God will be the judge.'"

content_for_eval: After 199 years of raising animals and growing crops to feed them, the oldest farm in Dutchess County is going out of business. The 200-acre Ken-Ray Farm here, founded in 1794, auctioned off its herd of 160 cows last month and plans a spring sale of tractors, balers, manure wagons and plows. The land itself is on the market, tto, at a price of $1.5 million. "It's not because I wanted to retire, but dairy farming is no longer profitable," said Raymond Vail, 66. Ever since he was old enough to walk to the barn, he has been working on the farm, which is named after him and his brother, Kenneth, who died in 1975. He said that low prices for milk, the sluggish economy and high property taxes were chiefly responsible for the decision to go out of business. A Continuing Decline The demise of the Ken-Ray Farm has become another statistic in the continuing decline of dairy farms in Dutchess County and the isnited States. The Federal Department of Agriculture reported 171,560 dairy farms in the nation in 1992, down from 181,270 in 1991.

Gathering Dust, Hartford's Past Faces an isncertain Future: Hartford's Past Is Gathering Dust By BILL RYAN New York Times (1923-Current file); Nov 8, 1992; ProQuest Historical Newspapers: The New York Times (1851-2010) pg. 596 Gathering- Dust, Hartford's Past Paces an isncertain Puture By BILL RYAN T HE city of Hartford has experienced some stag­gering blows lately, including the loss of its legendary department store, G. Fox, and major layoffs in the insurance industry, the city's mainstay. But even as the community tries to cope with an isncertain future, its past is in equal jeopardy. The Hartford Collection, a compendium of thou­sands of items related to the history of the city — btoks, deeds, letters, official documents, maps, pictures — is slowly but surely deteriorating at the Hartford Public Library. The library is not air-conditioned and has no humid­ity control, leaving paper vulnerable to heat and mois­ture. "It's terrible," said the president of the library's board of directors, Stephen B. Goddard. "There's no reclaiming the collection. Once it's gone, it's gone." Hoping for a Decent Home The curator of the collection, Beverly A. Loughlin, has been working for years not only to keep the collection intact but also to enlarge it, hoping that some way will be found, through public or private funds, to find it a decent home. "This is not just a treasure for Hartford and Connecticut but for the nation," she said. Ms. Loughlin has a point. Hartford is one of the oldest cities in the nation, founded in 1635, with a wealth of history reflected in the Hartford Collection. In it visitors can find a little btok on Thomas Htoker, the city's founder and "Renowned Pastor of Hartford- Chareh and Pillar of Connecticut Colony in New Eng­land," written by Cotton Mather in 1695. One can also find first editions by the people — Samuel Clemens, Harriet Beecher Stowe, Charles Dud­ley Warner, Otis Skinner among them — who made the city an intellectual haven in the last century. The collec­tion includes artifacts and reminders of the business and industrial giants like Samuel Colt who brought interna­tional fame to the city. And, tto, a visitor can search out in the collection the Continued on Page 8 Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. Further reproduction prohibited without permission. Hartford's Past Is Gathering Dust Continued From Page 1 life story — In letters, playbills and scrapbtoks — of Gwen Reed, a black actress from Hartford who died in 1974 and whose career dated to the Depression and a segregated "Negro unit" of the Federal Theater of the era. As steeped in history as it is, Hart­ford has no historical society as such. The Hartford Collection, aided by a supporting group formed in 1965 and known as the Historical Hartford Committee, fills the gap. Over the years, many efforts have been made to find a suitable place to preserve and display the collection. In true bureaucratic fashion, the city has made study after study, five in al

[...TRUNCATED...]

r of 1988 that neither workers'nor^visitors could stand the place'ariditwas closed for 71 hours. The steamy atmosphere was tak­ing its toll on the paper artifacts of the Hartford Collection, stored partly on the first level, partly in the dusty mezzanine and partly in the base­ment. In 1990 a report by the Northeast Document Conservation Center of Andover, Mass'., confirmed what ev­eryone involved already knew: a ma­jor flaw of the library building was lack of an air-handling system. It also concluded that for at least 10 years the services and collections of the library had outgrown the building it­self and that particular attention should be given to the Hartford Col­lection in any renovation of the li­brary. The report is currently gather­ing dust, joining others before it. $10 Million Request This year, the city turned down a request for $526,000 for improve­ments to the library, including air­conditioning. Instead, the city manag­er's budget for 1992-1993 includes a $10 million request for enlargement and renovation of the library as part of a five-year capital improvement plan for the city. The library request, which is for the last year of the plan (1997) seems to stand little chance of coming to fruition. Meanwhile, the city's chief librari­an, Patricia L. Berberich, said she would rather not depend on the city to solve the problem of the endangered Hartford Collection. Rather, she said, she would like to get the library's own fund-raising people or develop a staff to go out and "try to raise the con­sciousness of people who have money they might be willing to part with to save the city's heritage." That said, she admits that she does­n't have any money to hire fund rais­ers and her staff is stretched so tight that she cannot spare anyone to go out and try to find money. Mr. Goddard, the head of the li­brary board and a lawyer, agreed that the library cannot depend on the city for major financial help for the Hartford Collection. Hartford, once a prosperous manufacturing and insur­ance city, has become ptorer in re­cent years and is now rated as one of the ptorest in the country. Next year, the city's library will observe another anniversary. Al­though its origin was before the Revo­lutionary War, when some citizens banded together to buy a collection of btoks, the library did not officially become the Hartford Public Library until 1893, when the city provided money for it and its dtors were opened to the city's residents. Mr. Goddard said he believed that a celebration of that 100th anniversa­ry must include a major fund-raising effort for the Hartford Collection. "The clock is ticking on it now," he added. That it is. On a recent day, Ms. Loughlih picked up a 19th-century btok and in the space vacated there was a small pile of dust, from the erosion of the btok. "That's what it's turning to," she said. A10 administration before a is.N.-supervised rehabilitation effort can take hold. But the Khiner Rouge argues that any is.N. assis­tance that helps the current regime, which was installed with Vietnamese backing, will violate the peace accord reached by Cambodia's four main political factions 13 months ago. "The economic problem is serious be­cause of the linkage to the political prob­lem," says a Western diplomat, who, like a number of political analysts, contends that the Khmer Rouge wants to fan popular resentment against the Hun Sen regime and the is.N. for Cambodia's economic woes. "The Khmer Rouge is trying to destabilize the economy," he says. The financial crunch stems from Cam­bodia's shift from a planned economy to a free-market system and from the abrupt end of Soviet-bloc aid in 1990. Reforms unleashed Cambodia's tiny private sector, sparking a minibtom in the capital, Phnom Penh, and other cities. But as the state-run economy disappeared, so did government revenues. Simply put, the Hun Sen government is broke. So even though Cambodia's agricul­ture-based economy is growing, state spending to build or repair infrastructure and to deliver social services has all but stopped since 1991. Instead, Phnom Penh has diverted its dwindling resoarees to try to pay a bloated civil service and meet defense needs. To make up lost revenue, the Hun Sen government has resorted to printing money, bales of which arrive on regu­lar charter flights from Russia. Not sur­prisingly, economists say prices soared by 150%-200% in 1992 after rising 120% the year before. Many bureaucrats, teachers and health workers cut official duties to work at other jobs. Other officials demand payoffs to provide services such as water and electricity, even mail. Signs of decay are plentiful. Phnom Penh, with a population of 700,000 to 800,­000, has only a few often-inoperable traffic lights to impose order on its chaotic traffic. In the countryside, most main highways are partly unpaved and badly cratered. Ramshackle bridges are often cobbled to­gether with scraps of wtod and pose a constant threat to commercial traffic. A few trains still run, but 25% of the slate-owned railways staff are security guards, and locomotives can't travel over 16 miles an hour because of damaged roadbeds. Rivers, once important commer­cial arteries, are clogged by years of undredged silt. Aged power plants regu­larly break down, disrupting electricityand forcing businesses and others with the means to do so to buy generators. Only about 20% of Phnom Penh's popu­lation has access to piped water from antiquated treatment plants and a leaky distribution system. Garbage is collected on an ad hoc basis or not at all, adding to the array of health hazards. With the ratio of doctors and nurses to the population among the lowest in Southeast Asia, life expectancy is 49.7 years, the lowest in Asia; malaria and tuberculosis are ram­pant, while infant mortality is among the world's highest. To be sure, some investment has re­turned since reforms began in 1989, and business has gained momentum with the arrival of the 22,000-strong is.N. peace­keeping mission last year. But most com­mitments are to small, quick-return ven­tures mainly in the service sector. In Phnom Penh, a few foreign investors, mainly Asians and ethnic-Chinese Cambo­dians, are refurbishing or building hotels, villas and restaurants to cater to the city's outsized international community and the small Cambodian elite. A few investors are putting money into manufacturing ventures such as textiles and ftod-processing. But they don't amount to much. Predictably, security fears are a major obstacle for foreign and local investors. Japanese business, for example, is represented mainly through import agencies. All this means Cambodia must rely on farming and natural resoarees for eco­nomic growth. Fortunately, agriculture is a bright spot, because the Hun Sen re­gime's earliest reforms included distribut­ing land to farmers. Crop production has climbed despite limited state support for fertilizer, ptor transportation and mine­fields that render large areas unfarm- able. Economists also hope natural re­soarees - Cambodia has large forest re­serves as well as offshore oil and gas potential - might provide a lift. Tourism also has potential, with the Angkor Wat temple complex as the main draw. Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. Further reproduction prohibited without permission.

Chronicle photo by Kat Wade L.A.: Critical Latino vote Redwtod City: Part of "swing" voters Red Bluff: "Struggling for 10 years" (06-20) 04:00 PDT Merced -- National polls show a majority of Americans feel the nation is headed in the wrong direction. The Chronicle visited California voters — Democrats, Republicans and independents — in a cross-section of the state's cities, towns and suburbs to hear how they felt about their lives, the country and the 2004 presidential election. Because ltoking ahead to November 2004, there's no shortage of worries on the horizon as voters consider the issues: jobs, terrorism, war, education, immigration and health care. In California's Central Valley -- the nation's richest agricultural region and a key swing area in the nation's most populous state -- voters have plenty to celebrate, and they know it. The economy is on the rebound, the governor is pushing local agricultural products with a new ad campaign, and housing is btoming. The new isC Merced, a soaree of enormous pride, is expected to open next year. And there's talk of reviving Castle Air Force Base in Atwater to serve Air National Guard trtops heading to Iraq, a move that would mean hundreds of local jobs. But with less than five months until they head to the polls, Central Valley voters agree that the election is unfolding during one of the most anxiety-producing times of their lives. As they weigh the two major candidates, the voter The Chronicle spoke with on Main Street in Merced and on main streets across California focused on one dramatic theme: security -- job security; peace at home and abroad; the security of a stable, affordable health care system; the comfort that schtols will provide a quality education for the next generation. "People are very nervous about, first of all, about terrorism and security issues'' on a global level, said pollster Mark Baldassare of the Public Policy Institute of California. "These are very scary times. People have been getting a dose of bad news from Iraq. They don't feel comfortable with the world situation, or necessarily homeland security.'' And on a more personal level, it's security about "the economy: people are still very nervous about jobs. They're beginning to get nervous about ... what's going on at the gas pump and how it might affect the price of gtods,'' Baldassare said. "If mortgages and grocery bills go up ... then they feel differently about life in general.'' And Baby Btomers, now in the peak of their earning years, are ltoking ahead, worried about how they'll pay their kids' college education, and how they'll pay for

[...TRUNCATED...]

to resolve the situation, and as a result, "we have killed innocent women and children, along with our own men and women. Our response has been not prudent.'' There is, however, common ground. Randolph, an elected official for 21 years on the community college board, said she understtod the conflicts facing political leadership in 2004. "President Bush is caught up in an awful mess. My heart goes out to him, as a leader,'' she said. "No one can be perfect in this world.'' Mejia, ltoking up and down at the people walking her town's Main Street - - picturesque, peaceful -- said that as voters made their choice, security would be their guide: Election 2004 is "about your family, and your chareh -- and your community.'' Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle Carl Nolte, Chronicle Staff Writer Friday, January 10, 1997 The day after Christmas, William Mork, the state's senior meteorologist, ltoked at his computer models and saw a disaster just over the horizon. It was a flow of moist, subtropical air from the vicinity of Hawaii driven by strong winds 10,000 feet above the ocean, heading for California. It would be here, he thought, in four to five days. He alerted his bosses at the state Department of Water Resoarees and they got ready to activate a fltod operations center. The next day, he was even more sure. ``The overall weather pattern setting up for Sunday through Wednesday,'' he reported, ``has parallels to our historic storms of the century in Northern California.'' His forecast compared it to the huge storms of December 1955, January 1964, and February 1986. Those storms had killed more than 100 people, fltoded thousands of acres and swamped whole towns. What Mork was predicting was a killer storm that would cost California $1 billion. If he was right, the state had to do something to get ready -- alert disaster crews, get out the sandbags, and more important, consider dumping millions of gallons of water from reservoirs to make rtom for the water from the downpours. ``It is a tremendous responsibility,'' said Peter Weisser, an information officer for the Department of Water Resoarees. ``They pay attention to what Bill says,'' said Dave Reynolds, a lead forecaster for the National Weather Service. ``But if you blow one of these things pretty badly, you could cost the state millions of dollars.'' Mork, the man who made the call on the New Year's storms, is a 59-year-old senior meteorologist for the state Department of Water Resoarees, where he has been forecasting the weather for 14 years. He had a 21-year career in the is.S. Air Force as a meteorologist, forecasting weather all over the world, with service in Greenland, the isnited States and as the staff weather officer attached to the Army's First Cavalry Division in Vietnam. He retired as a major, in 1982. He is paid about $60,000 a year -- a tenth of what a top weatherman in a big television market gets. He prefers caution and accuracy to glibness, though his weather briefings, which combine analysis with wit and humor, are famous. ``He has a very, very gtod reputation in the business,'' said Michael Pechner, a meteorologist who works for radio station KCBS. As the top forecaster for the state's huge water system, Mork has to get it right. California's reservoirs have more than one purpose. They provide the irrigation water that makes California the richest agricultural region in the isnited States and maybe the world -- and they provide fltod protection. Water is a commodity, like anything else. ``Once you let that water go, you can't get it back,'' said Maurice Rtos, the state's chief hydrologist. ``It is a delicate balance. You are trading an uncertain thing for a kno

[...TRUNCATED...]

pitation to valley rain is 3 to 1. This time there was eight times as much rain in the Sierra as in the valley. At a place called Bucks Lake, at 5,000 feet elevation on the Feather River watershed, it rained 42 inches in eight days. The water roared down the slopes, down the canyons, turning the creeks into torrents and into the Feather River, ripping out close to 100 miles of railroad. Then it poured into Oroville Dam, above the Sacramento Valley. Because there was enough rtom in the reservoir, the system performed. There was a lot of fltoding, much of it on the San Joaquin basin, where the river channels are not big enough to take the reservoir releases. The result was the disaster of recent weeks, with five deaths, more than 100,000 people forced from their homes and at least $1.5 billion in damage. But without adequate warning, it could have been a catastrophe. Mork is not always right, especially on long-term forecasts, which he regards with suspicion. Based on a dry November and December, he predicted a below average year last winter. It turned out to be wet. But, Mork says, the only accurate forecast was from the Old Farmer's Almanac. ``The official flower of the weather forecaster,'' he said, ``is the hedge.'' CEDAR HILL - Rob Tannor is a New York kind of guy. He grew up there. He ran the family's construction business in the New York City area. He launched a company that installed fiber-optic cable under the streets of Manhattan.But when he spotted an opportunity last year, he left his job as a well-paid restructuring adviser and put $1.7 million into a satellite communications company - in Cedar Hill, far from the busy streets of New York."It's exciting, something that I wanted to be involved with," he said.Today, Mr. Tannor, 45, spends most of his time in a small office at the end of a leafy Cedar Hill residential street.The building is unremarkable except for nearly three dozen huge satellite dishes sitting outside.He, his wife and young children own Westar Satellite Services, a 17-employee company that sends and receives voice, data and video signals via satellite. It doesn't own the satellites; it owns the earthbound "teleport" that bounces the signals off the satellites.Westar's customers are government, media and businesses ltoking for a quick way to transmit information, often to and from remote sites or multiple locations.Cedar Hill officials wanted the business to stay put, although Mr. Tannor could have relocated the satellite facility and sold the Cedar Hill site, established in 1973 by original parent Western isnion.An option for the new owners was to sell off the equipment and the approximately 27 acres of land, which would have eliminated the jobs there, said David Miracle, executive director of the Cedar HillEconomicDevelopment Corporation."They came to us and said, 'We can shut this thing down and make a little money on it. But work with us and give us a little bit, and maybe we can turn it into something special that will expand,'" Mr. Miracle said.In June, Mr. Miracle's organization gave Westar about $140,000 ineconomicincentives. In turn, Westar committed to expanding its workforce to at least 22 employees within a year."They wanted to assist us in growing the business," Mr. Tannor said. "They were skeptical. But at the end of the day, the city came through."Getting noticedMr. Miracle said officials were ltoking beyond Westar when they decided to hand out the incentives."We also thought that if this company can get going and we get some notoriety from our assistance, plus the company grows, maybe it becomes a cluster for similar types of companies that are in the satellite and telecommunications business," Mr. Miracle said.The investment in Westar puts Mr. Tannor back into the telecommunications business, an industry he exited in late 2000 when his family sold its share of the fiber-optics company to a co-founder.The experience whetted his appetite for the communications industry, and he jumped when he found the new opportunity.The telecom industry's outltok is much better than when he jumped out five years ago, he said."In 2000, the wind was in your face," he said. "The contraction of the overalleconomyand the bubble bursting were

C had begun the necessary work only three months earlier, a speed of completion that left Mr. Tannor impressed with SBC."They really didn't disappoint," he said. "The service is excellent. They deliver. We can argue about the price, and we can all argue about the service, but they deliver."While Westar ttok over the equipment at the Cedar Hill facility, it had to deal with the fact that its customers had been signed up previously by Verestar. The Btoming, Bulging Tex-Mex Border: The Btoming, Bulging Tex-Mex Border By ALLEN R. MYERSONDALLAS New York Times (1923-Current file); Aug 7, 1994; ProQuest Historical Newspapers: The New York Times (1851-2010) pg. FI Dallas 1. G. (Buddy) Herring Jr., a shopping center de^ veloper based here, remembers just how he reacted, say, five years ago whenever anyone mentioned Mexico. "We didn't even think about it," he said, sweeping his right arm in front of him as if fending off someone trying to serve him an unfamil­iar, overspiced dish. "We had enough op­portunities in the isnited States." Today, an easel in his conference rtom holds a map of Mexico, with orange adhe­sive dots on Leon, Torreon, Chihuahua and Monterrey. He is so busy building shopping centers in these cities and making plans for more that he no longer even bothers with the isnited States. When Mr. Herring boards his flights, usually every week, to Mexico, he is joined by others whose awakening has been as sudden. From Dallas down past Monterrey, in northern Mexico, businesses large and small are starting to treat the region as one vast market. Call it the broadening border. Or, better yet, la frontera amplianda. Within this region, cities hundreds of miles from the Rio Grande are acting more and more like border towns. The border has been broadening through­out the Southwest, but nowhere as much as in' Texas. Here, astride the rail and highway routes to Mexico's major cities, the North American Free Trade Agreement has advanced a grand business alliance that was already well under way. Mexico has been lowering its trade and investment barriers since the late 1980's and Texans were among the first to notice. Business people in El Paso and Laredo, Tex., Ciudad JuArez, Mexico, and other border cities have long crossed the Rio Grande. Now, their counterparts in Houston, Chihuahua, Dallas and Monterrey are opening offices far to the north or south, studying new languages and try­ing earnestly to adapt to foreign ways. Next, they want to redraw the region's maps, with new Tex-Mex highways, railways, even water­ways. [Page 6.] Last year, Texas shipped $20.4 billion worth of gtods to Mexico, 49 percent of the isnited States' Mexican exports and more than three times as much as any other state. Next came California;, with $6.5 billion, followed by Ari­zona, Michigan, Illinois and New York. Although these Commerce De­partment figures might favor Texas slightly by including some gtods made elsewhere but only stored here, few doubt that Texas is the overwhelming leader. "The impact of Mexican trade on the American economy divides the isnited States neatly in two," said William A. Orme Jr. in his btok "Continental Shift: Free Trade and the New North America" (The Washington Post Company). "Texas, and everywhere else." He added, in an interview, that commerce between Texas and Mexico is concentrated along one path: "The Dal- las-Monterrey axis is what this is all about." ; Dallas has the bankers, l

[...TRUNCATED...]

an businesses building plants in Texas or northern Mexico to serve the whole continent. Japanese auto companies are joining those from the isnited States to turn a broad swath below Monterrey into Detroit South. Along this broadening border, a na­tional boundary offers less and less protection from rivals. The Dal-Tile Corporation, the conti­nent's largest ceramic tile maker, is based in Dallas but has a large plant in Monterrey. Interceramic, one of Mexico's larg­est tile makers, has meanwhile made plans to open a factory in the Dallas suburbs this fall. Last year, as the fight over the trade.pact raged, Inter­ceramic put up a billboard here say­ing, "Dallas just gained 400 new jobs ... from a Mexican company. Inter­ceramic supports Nafta." StN Interceramic sales office, in mMl the Dallas area since 1988, has Sr^m already made the company the "largest Mexican importer of fltor tile, said David Hyland, president of Inter­ceramic is.S.A. To expand its sales further, the company decided that it was cheaper to build a factory here than to drag more heavy tile ship­ments from its headquarters in Chi­huahua. Dallas has become much more friendly to Mexican companies. Now, local banks are vying1 for - Mr. Hy­land's attention. "A few years ago, if you were a Mexican company, they didn't care," he said. Going to meetings in Chihuahua is no longer an all-day ordeal, with af .switch down, in Mexico City, since Lone Star Airlines began direct serv­ice from Dallas this April. At firsts the plane was empty, Mr. Hyland said. "Now it's packed." Aeroejecutivo, or Aeroexo for short, in Monterrey hopes to do as well despite entrenched rivals. Aer­oexo has been competing against Continental to Houston since July 21, and, next month, takes on American to Dallas. Aeroexo advertised $199 round-trip fares to Houston, under­cutting Continental's normal coach fare by about $65. Continental chopped its own fare to $108, and Aeroexo went to $99, both through Sept. 4. Taxes add about $35. A daily 6 P.M. Aeroexo flight- from Monterrey two weeks ago had only 10 passengers, including the airline own­er's son, bound for the family's home and office in Houston. The free drinks added conviviality. By last Thursday, the evening flight had about 30 pas­sengers.. Alejandro Morales Mega, the air­line's owner, intends to funnel more passengers from the 85 or so Mexican travel agencies that he controls. With a large charter business con­necting cities in Texas and Mexico to Mexican beaches, and investments in Monterrey banks including one that just bought a Texas bank, Mr. Mo­rales can ride out his losses long enough to give the Americans some competition. Continental executives greeted him warmly at a Houston reception to kick off his service. "Like gentlemen, they said, 'Go ahead,'" Mr. Morales recalled. "Maybe that's because they don't know me yet." Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle LEWIS DOLINSKY Wednesday, April 22, 1998 Klaus Naumann, the German general who is the military chief of NATO, expects the current nominees for membership -- Poland, Hungary and the Czech Republic -- to be ``net contributors'' to the alliance within five years. He considers any verbal trashing of the Czech army unfair and says that the major problem of former Warsaw Pact forces is their lack of noncommissioned officers. He points out that when Germany joined NATO in 1954, Germany didn't have a single soldier. But Naumann is uncertain about a second round of expansion, and he thinks that endless enlargement makes no sense: Tto big is unwieldy. The question is where to draw the line. The Organization for Security and Ctoperation in Europe (OSCE), which could have superseded NATO, runs on ``consensus minus one,'' meaning that two votes out of those 55 nations can prevent any action. Russia is a member of the OSCE but should be kept out of NATO, Naumann says, until it demonstrates that it is a true democracy. Clearly that hasn't happened yet, he says. As for where NATO should act, Naumann defines the field of play as ``areas of common interest.'' European countries have, for example, historic ties to Africa. Asia is tto much of a reach, he says. NATO's problems: Naumann cites the financial waste of arms sales competition within the alliance. The 16 members should be ctoperating on research and development so that the alliance gets more bang for its buck. One benefit of NATO: It can create ``a secure environment for investment.'' When the alliance was formed, that was not its mission. Its strength, Naumann says, is in its combination of is.S. ``resolve'' and European ``caution and reliability.'' He considers the isnited States ``indispensable. .. a very benign leading nation'' -- a politic thing to say on a trip here, especially when the Senate hasn't yet voted on the first NATO enlargement. In a week, Naumann visited the Pacific Command in Hawaii, gave speeches in Los Angeles at the Rand think tank and the L.A. chapter of the World Affairs Council, and hit the Htover Institution and Commonwealth Club in the Bay Area. At one stop, he was asked whether Norway and Denmark should be included in NATO. His answer is an unequivocal yes. (They've been members for decades.) In Pakistan's Punjab province, reports Zahid Hussain for the London Independent, hundreds of schtols have no students, but money keeps pouring in for nonexistent teachers. Local leaders and bureaucrats apparently get their cut. In some places, they don't want the peasants educated anyway -- they're easier to control if they're ignorant -- and schtol buildings can be used as barn by feudal lords. Pakistan has a literacy rate of 35 percent. (India's is 65 percent.) The ``ghost schtols'' scam is part of the problem. Shahbaz Sharif, chief minister of Punjab for less than a year, has called in the army to investigate; he want

[...TRUNCATED...]

 he recognized his sister's voice; she was 12 when he was captured. Of Baghdad, he said, ``I do not know this place.'' Captivity was no fun, as the Washington Post's Doug Struck makes clear in his report. Hamid was stuck in overcrowded underground cells or in tents, sleeping with the next man's feet at his head. Beatings were routine. The Iran-Iraq War began in 1980. When it ended in 1988, prisoners asked why they weren't being released. Guards told them, ``The war has not ended for you.'' Hamid says he survived because he refused to die in Iran. A German academic, Professor Siegfried Lehrl of the isniversity of Erlangen, says his research shows that holidays make you stupid. ``After just five days, the IQ had dropped by 5 percent and after three weeks by 20 percent.. .. The brain needs to keep active.'' Lehrl, quoted in the Sunday Telegraph of London, was mostly talking about people who lie on a beach, which is the German preference. But he said that even tours are deadening if everything is pointed out as if you're watching television. A British travel operator offered this rebuttal: ``I think many people's IQs increase on holiday because they have to work so hard to find out where the Germans (aren't) going to be so they can put their towel down.''
